bins.name: Study load with generation
bins.desc: Microsoft backs studying large loads and generation together, arguing it speeds interconnection, improves grid planning, and can shrink the scale and cost of transmission upgrades. It frames the most useful reforms as those that boost speed-to-market and cut network-upgrade cost and scope.
quotes: Microsoft believes studying load and generation together has the potential to speed up the interconnection process for load and generation by eliminating duplicative, and potentially conflicting, studies and will facilitate more efficient grid planning by more closely aligning generation capacity needs with large loads.
Additionally, studying load and generation together potentially reduces the scale and cost of transmission upgrades by minimizing system impacts and the need for major backbone reinforcements.

In Microsoft’s view, the changes ripe for consideration are those that (i) focus on processes that will increase speed-to-market for new customers and new generation and (ii) reduce the cost and scope of network upgrades needed for interconnection.

bins.name: Standardized study requirements
bins.desc: Microsoft supports standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to add regulatory certainty, deter speculation, and improve transparency. It wants processes that clearly state study requirements, financial commitments, timelines, and the duties of both utility and customer.
quotes: Microsoft supports standardizing study requirements to provide greater regulatory certainty, deter speculation, and enhance transparency so businesses can plan with more predictability.
Interconnection processes should clearly articulate study requirements, financial commitments, timelines, and responsibilities of the utility and the customer.

bins.name: Expedited curtailable study
bins.desc: Microsoft agrees voluntarily curtailable loads make sense and supports an expedited study process for curtailable loads and dispatchable hybrid facilities. It conditions that support on not creating reliability risks and not interfering with interconnection of critical nonflexible large loads.
quotes: Microsoft agrees that regulatory frameworks that allow for voluntarily curtailable loads make sense and will be an important tool for accelerating the deployment of critical AI infrastructure.

Microsoft believes an expedited study process for curtailable loads and dispatchable hybrid facilities would be appropriate and consistent with the interests of the administration, so long as these arrangements do not create operational reliability risks and do not interfere with the interconnection of critical nonflexible large loads.

bins.name: Network-upgrade cost and credits
bins.desc: Microsoft accepts responsibility for its share of network-upgrade costs and supports transparent calculation so neighbors and the local community do not subsidize it. Where up-front-funded upgrades broadly benefit other system users, it favors a crediting mechanism, possibly modeled on generator-only interconnection credits.
quotes: Microsoft supports the work of utilities and regulators to make sure these costs are transparently calculated and assigned to it and the other customers who benefit from those capital investments. This work helps ensure that Microsoft’s neighbors and the local community do not pay for Microsoft’s share of the costs.

If the network upgrades (with costs paid for up-front by the interconnecting loads they are assigned to) are providing broad benefits to other system users—for example, if another industrial customer benefits from the same infrastructure —Microsoft believes it would be fair to use a crediting mechanism or other arrangement that would capture the reliability and economic value those network upgrades provide other electricity consumers.
One such approach may be similar to the crediting mechanisms used for interconnecting generator-only facilities.

bins.name: No position on jurisdiction
bins.desc: Microsoft explicitly takes no position on whether the Commission should exercise jurisdiction over load interconnections.
quotes: As noted above, Microsoft takes no position on whether the Commission should exercise jurisdiction over load interconnections.

bins.name: Standardized study process
bins.desc: Microsoft wants standardized, transparent study processes that remove uncertainty and reduce network-upgrade cost and scope. It also notes study reform could make system-level load forecasting more consistent for planners.
quotes: Microsoft believes studying load and generation together has the potential to speed up the interconnection process for load and generation by eliminating duplicative, and potentially conflicting, studies and will facilitate more efficient grid planning by more closely aligning generation capacity needs with large loads.

In Microsoft’s view, the changes ripe for consideration are those that (i) focus on processes that will increase speed-to-market for new customers and new generation and (ii) reduce the cost and scope of network upgrades needed for interconnection.
Microsoft supports standardizing study requirements to provide greater regulatory certainty, deter speculation, and enhance transparency so businesses can plan with more predictability.
Interconnection processes should clearly articulate study requirements, financial commitments, timelines, and responsibilities of the utility and the customer.
An additional benefit of interconnection study reform is that it could bring greater consistency to system-level load forecasting for system planners.

bins.name: Cost allocation and transparency
bins.desc: Microsoft supports fair, transparent cost allocation, accepting its share while wanting to avoid shifting costs to neighbors. It favors crediting mechanisms when up-front-funded upgrades deliver broad benefits, and sees joint studies as a way to lower transmission-upgrade costs.
quotes: Additionally, studying load and generation together potentially reduces the scale and cost of transmission upgrades by minimizing system impacts and the need for major backbone reinforcements.

In Microsoft’s view, the changes ripe for consideration are those that (i) focus on processes that will increase speed-to-market for new customers and new generation and (ii) reduce the cost and scope of network upgrades needed for interconnection.

Microsoft supports the work of utilities and regulators to make sure these costs are transparently calculated and assigned to it and the other customers who benefit from those capital investments. This work helps ensure that Microsoft’s neighbors and the local community do not pay for Microsoft’s share of the costs.

If the network upgrades (with costs paid for up-front by the interconnecting loads they are assigned to) are providing broad benefits to other system users—for example, if another industrial customer benefits from the same infrastructure —Microsoft believes it would be fair to use a crediting mechanism or other arrangement that would capture the reliability and economic value those network upgrades provide other electricity consumers.
One such approach may be similar to the crediting mechanisms used for interconnecting generator-only facilities.

bins.name: Flexible-load services
bins.desc: Microsoft supports frameworks for voluntarily curtailable loads as a tool to accelerate AI infrastructure, while noting most data centers today have limited curtailment capability. It asks regulators and grid operators to work with data-center operators to define clear program parameters such as maximum curtailment times, frequency, and size.
quotes: Microsoft agrees that regulatory frameworks that allow for voluntarily curtailable loads make sense and will be an important tool for accelerating the deployment of critical AI infrastructure.
As discussed briefly below, most data centers today have limited curtailment capability.

Microsoft believes an expedited study process for curtailable loads and dispatchable hybrid facilities would be appropriate and consistent with the interests of the administration, so long as these arrangements do not create operational reliability risks and do not interfere with the interconnection of critical nonflexible large loads.

Microsoft encourages regulators, utilities, and grid operators—with consultation from datacenter operators—to create program clarity by identifying the type of flexibility that would bring value to the electric system and developing program parameters (such as maximum load curtailment times, frequency, and size) that are critical for datacenters to consider when making decisions about program participation.

bins.name: System load forecasting
bins.desc: Microsoft sees a side benefit of study reform in more consistent system-level load forecasting, letting large loads validate the load levels planners use.
quotes: An additional benefit of interconnection study reform is that it could bring greater consistency to system-level load forecasting for system planners.

bins.name: Limited data-center curtailment
bins.desc: Microsoft notes that most data centers today have limited curtailment capability, a caveat on how much flexible-load programs can deliver near-term.
quotes: As discussed briefly below, most data centers today have limited curtailment capability.

bins.name: Curtailment program design
bins.desc: Microsoft urges regulators, utilities, and grid operators to consult data-center operators and define clear curtailment program parameters so operators can decide whether to participate.
quotes: Microsoft encourages regulators, utilities, and grid operators—with consultation from datacenter operators—to create program clarity by identifying the type of flexibility that would bring value to the electric system and developing program parameters (such as maximum load curtailment times, frequency, and size) that are critical for datacenters to consider when making decisions about program participation.

bins.name: Transition plan stability
bins.desc: Microsoft supports transition plans that mitigate existing risk without creating new regulatory uncertainty, covering projects under study, those already built or paid for, and facilities phasing in or operating.
quotes: Microsoft is supportive of transition plans that mitigate existing risk and do not create new regulatory risk or uncertainty for projects. A transition plan should provide regulatory stability for large loads that are already under study; large loads for which the study process has already been completed, payments made, and construction commenced; and for facilities that are phasing in or are fully operational.
